text: The surgeon confirms the location of the splenic flexure tumor. Traction is applied to the inferior mesenteric vein, and a plane is created between the mesocolon and the retroperitoneum. The inferior mesenteric vein is divided using a ligature. The dissection continues to develop a plane between the pancreas and the mesocolon of the splenic flexure, mobilizing the tail of the pancreas. Traction is then applied to the left branch of the middle colic artery, which is skeletalized, clipped, and divided with a ligasure. The remaining mesentery is divided, ensuring the removal of all lymphatic tissue associated with the left branch of the middle colic artery.

The surgeon mobilizes the descending colon mesentery in a medial to lateral fashion along the axis of the inferior mesenteric vein. Tension is applied to the inferior mesenteric artery pedicle, and a plane is developed between the artery and the urethra peritoneum. Tension is then applied to the trifurcation of the inferior mesenteric artery, superior hemorrhoidal artery, and left colic artery. The left colic artery and vein are skeletalized at their origin, clipped individually, and divided with ligature, completing the medial mobilization.
The surgeon focuses on lateral mobilization, clearly visualizing the ureter. The dissection progresses towards the spleen, and the omentum is separated from the transverse colon.

The surgeon completes the immobilization of the splenic flexure by carefully dissecting the mesentery and associated omentum from the tail of the pancreas, revealing the medial plane of dissection along the pancreas.
The fully exposed tail of the pancreas is visible. The surgical specimen is extracted through the umbilicus, resected, and an end-to-end anastomosis is performed. Perfusion is visually confirmed.
